solution.label: Loess Retirement: Micro-Particle Accumulation
solution.design_principles: Eliminate contribution decisions entirely. Make saving invisible, automatic, and proportional to economic activity. Treat income variability as natural terrain to flow with, not fight against.
solution.core_mechanism: Every financial transaction deposits microscopic retirement 'particles' (0.1-0.5% of value) into a shared watershed pool. Your retirement draw rights equal your lifetime particle contributions as a share of total pool value.
solution.how_it_works: 1) All payment processors automatically split micro-amounts to retirement pool (like loess particles accumulating from wind). 2) Contributions happen on both income and spending—volatility in either direction still generates particles. 3) Pool invests collectively with professional management. 4) Draw rights begin at any age once contributions exceed threshold, converting gradually from contributor to drawer. 5) No monthly decisions, no 'missing' contributions during lean months—if money moves through your life, particles accumulate.
solution.what_is_new: Eliminates contribution timing decisions entirely. Traditional systems punish inconsistency; this makes consistency irrelevant. You can't fail to contribute because contribution is embedded in economic participation itself.
solution.why_it_works: Palouse loess soil became Earth's most fertile through 2 million years of dust. Similarly, decision-free micro-accumulation removes the psychological barrier that defeats most variable-income savers. When you can't choose not to save, income volatility stops mattering.
solution.why_it_fails: Requires universal payment infrastructure participation—cash economies and informal work remain invisible. Could also reduce worker awareness of retirement, leading to insufficient supplementary saving when possible. The 'shared pool' creates redistribution politics.
solution.medium_term: Gig workers accumulate retirement without noticing. The 'retirement crisis' category shifts from 'didn't save' to 'didn't earn enough lifetime'—clarifying that the problem was always income, not discipline.
solution.long_term_vision: Retirement disappears as a distinct life phase. Instead, everyone has a continuously-building draw right they can tap flexibly. Work patterns become genuinely fluid—taking a year off to care for family or pursue art no longer means 'losing retirement years.' Economic participation itself, not employment status, builds security.
